answer:
In the Old English elegies compiled in the *Old English Elegies* collection—such as *The Wanderer*, *Maxims I*, *Vainglory*, *Resignation*, and the *Instructions for a Freeborn Son* (*Maxims II*)—"wisdom" (often rendered via *frod* or *witig*, alongside related forms like *wīsdōm* or *snottor*) denotes a hard-earned, practical prudence shaped by age, suffering, and reflection on fate (*wyrd*), transience (*eorþan ymbhwyrft*), and moral order. It emphasizes restraint, counsel, endurance amid exile or loss, and insight into the world's impermanence, frequently contrasted with folly (*dwǣlan*, hot-headedness). This wisdom is gnomic and communal: shared through maxims, fatherly teaching, or solitary rumination, often invoking divine or cosmic stability as a model.[^1][^2][^3][^4]

For instance, in *The Wanderer*, wisdom (*frod in ferþe*) arises from weathering "his share of winters," manifesting as patience (*hrēowcearig*), moderation in speech/action, and deep pondering of ruins/slaughter, enabling one to perceive ghostly *wyrd* without despair.[^5] *Maxims I* opens with a call to exchange "wise words" (*frodum wordum*) openly, linking wisdom to praising God, recognizing mortality, ethical pairings (e.g., "the wise should hold council with the wise"), and proper social/natural order; it resides "in the breast" (*on brēostum*) as restrained thought.[^6] *Vainglory* begins "Hwaet, me frod wita... saegde" ("Listen, a wise sage [*frod wita*] told me"), contrasting humble wisdom with vainglory/envy.[^7] The *Instructions* repeatedly feature a "wise father" (*frod fæder*) imparting *wīsdōm* as moral vigilance: avoiding wickedness, drunkenness/envy, choosing good over evil, and guarding speech/thoughts.[^8] Even God is "witig dryhten" in *Deor*, turning *wyrd* providentially.[^9]

Cynewulfian poems (*Andreas*, *Elene*, *Juliana*) retain this experiential prudence but Christianize it: *frod*/*witig* connotes divinely inspired insight, fortitude against persecution, and discernment of truth amid deception, often in saints' trials. Wisdom grows with divine word (*word ond wīsdōm*), enabling victory over fiends/flesh.[^10][^11]

In *Andreas*, priests are "witig werede" (wise host) proclaiming truth; Christ grows "word ond wīsdōm"; Andrew praises the helmsman's "greater wisdom" (*wīsdōm*) in seamanship as God-granted.[^12] *Elene* has David as "frod fyrnweota" (wise ancient counselor) prophesying; Elene presses "mind-wise" (*frod*) Jews for wisdom they unwisely discarded.[^13] *Juliana* deems her tormentor "on ferþe frod" (wise in mind) only if shamed before God.[^14]

**Comparison**: Elegiac wisdom is stoic/pragmatic—age-tempered endurance of *wyrd*, gnomic counsel for social harmony, wary of excess (e.g., "not too hot-hearted nor too hasty"). Cynewulf elevates it to hagiographic discernment: active resistance to idols/devils via faith, where prudence reveals Christ as true *wīsdōm*-source. Both value interiority ("in ferþe/brēost"), communal exchange, and divine anchor, but elegies stress fatalistic reflection, Cynewulf triumphant agency.[^15]

[^1]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 6 (*Maxims I*), [translation](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/06-maxims-i/translation/#p-84cf09).
[^2]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 12 (*Vainglory*), [translation](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/12-vainglory/translation/#p-946b37).
[^3]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 17 (*Instructions*), [translation](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/17-thus-frod-faeder-freobearn-laerde/translation/#p-7ec4d3).
[^4]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 9 (*Resignation*), [translation](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/09-resignation/translation/#p-04f5b1).
[^5]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 11 (*The Wanderer*), [translation](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11-the-wanderer/translation/#p-9cca5e).
[^6]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 6 (*Maxims I*), [translation](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/06-maxims-i/translation/#p-84cf09).
[^7]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 12 (*Vainglory*), [translation](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/12-vainglory/translation/#p-946b37).
[^8]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 17 (*Instructions*), [translation](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/17-thus-frod-faeder-freobearn-laerde/translation/#p-7ec4d3).
[^9]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 13 (*Deor*), [translation](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/13-welund-him-be-wurman-wraeces-cunnade/translation/).
[^10]: *Andreas*, ch. 1, [translation](/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/#p-992aeb).
[^11]: *Elene*, ch. 1, [translation](/works/cynewulf-elene-d2d132/01/translation/#p-624562).
[^12]: *Andreas*, ch. 1, [translation](/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/#p-5b21c9).
[^13]: *Elene*, ch. 1, [translation](/works/cynewulf-elene-d2d132/01/translation/#p-7b1c1f).
[^14]: *Juliana*, ch. 1, [translation](/works/cynewulf-juliana-9a2157/01/translation/#p-01394b).
[^15]: Cf. wiki cards for structural overlaps (e.g., elegies nearest Cynewulfian works via TF-IDF: *Andreas* cosine 0.35).
